text: If you are a first time marathon runner, put your hands up, let's see you.
Look at these amazing people out here.
How proud do you think they are?
They're getting ready to finish their very first marathon.
My name is Lauren Kleppet and I just ran a 2.28.
Daniel Brinkenhoff, I'm a Los Angeles resident.
Yoshio Tahara coming from Japan.
My name is Lisa Hansen.
I'm a cancer survivor.
Victor Yebra, Astic Dance Group.
I'm a general contractor.
I'm a cardiologist.
I'm a student.
I'm a recovering alcoholic.
I am a mom and I am a daughter.
And I'm a father of four.
Mother of five.
I'm a mother and a mother to be.
My name is Chad and I'm an athlete.
I didn't really believe that in myself until today.
My name is Jose Maldonado and I'm a marathon runner.
My name is Sheva Wheeler, I'm 57, I'm a first time marathoner and I'm tired as hell.
I'm the only sixth grader in my family.
I'm from a middle school that runs the LA Marathon.
This is my 14th LA Marathon in this costume.
This is my first marathon barefoot.
My name is Charles Cutting and I've just finished my 29th LA Marathon.
My name is Jose Cornejo and I'm a Legacy Runner.
My name is Charles Sales and I am a 77 year old marathon runner.
Today I completed my 205th marathon.
Dig deep, just get done with this thing and don't collapse.
I just put it on turbo boost and I just ran all the way in.
Pulled muscles, strained muscles.
Everything started cramping.
I was doing everything inside of me to keep going.
My chemo took me five hours and when people say you can't do something I like to prove them wrong.
Your body starts yelling at you, what are you doing to me?
We've never done this before, why are you doing this?
How much longer are we going to do it?
And then it seems that your body just kind of finally realizes that we're in for the long haul.
I can't give up, I can't stop because I remember why I started.
Two years ago I couldn't run a mile.
To go from that to this is a challenge and here I am.
You get to that point in the race where you know that you're going to slow down
and you've just got to break through that mental barrier.
For me running is redemption and it's an important part of my recovery from alcoholism.
I was about 230 pounds, smoked every day.
My son was two years old and I thought it was time to grow up.
We all use each other when we need to reach deep.
It's the people along the way that just bring things to life.
I've never seen so much love and passion in the city before and it was really refreshing to see that
and that definitely helped me get through.
When you get to that finish line you know where you're at, you know how much you're given.
It's crazy unbelievable what your body can do and now I can feel like I can do anything.
I looked at the time and it was about three minutes slower than I wanted
but I'll take it for my first marathon.
I thought I could never do it and accomplish it and I just let it cry because I actually did it.
I never thought I could do it and so I'm going to move on to the triathlon next.
It's so exciting because I know the trials will be here in a couple years and racing for USA.
I didn't think I could do it at first. I'm just really proud of myself.
It always feels great to finish.
You really only have one opponent and that's your previous personal best
and I defeated that opponent today.
